sections.label: neurons in your brain, each doing something embarrassingly simple

sections.text: You have eighty-six billion neurons. And each one does exactly one thing: it receives signals, adds them up, and decides whether to fire. That is it. No single neuron knows what you are thinking. No single neuron understands language or love or music. But together, they create everything you have ever experienced.

sections.label: The Artificial Neuron
sections.subtitle: Inspired by biology, simplified by math

sections.text: An artificial neuron works the same way. It takes in numbers, multiplies each by a weight that says how important it is, adds them all up, and passes the result through a function that decides: should I activate or stay quiet? That is the entire secret of AI. Weighted sum. Activation. That is it.

steps.label: Inputs arrive
steps.detail: Numbers from sensors, pixels, or other neurons

steps.label: Multiply by weights
steps.detail: Each input has a learned importance score

steps.label: Output
steps.detail: Send signal to the next layer

sections.text: Here is the beautiful part. One neuron is useless. It can only draw a single straight line through data. But stack thousands together in layers and something magical happens. The first layer detects edges. The second layer combines edges into shapes. The third recognizes faces. Complexity emerges from simplicity.

When you talk to Claude or ask an AI to write a poem, you are sending signals through billions of these simple neurons, arranged in layers so deep they would fill a skyscraper. Each one does its tiny job. And somehow, understanding emerges. That is the miracle of neural networks.
Simplicity, stacked deep enough, becomes intelligence.
sections.subtext: That is what a neuron teaches us.

sections.heading: Next: How Neurons Learn
sections.subtext: The weights start random. Training makes them meaningful.
